name: Club of Dagda
description: The Club of Dagda, also known as the lorg mór (great club), is a legendary weapon associated with the Dagda, a prominent god in Irish mythology. This is no mere bludgeon; it is a symbol of immense power and control over life and death. The club is described as being so massive that it could kill nine men with a single blow, yet it could also restore them to life with the opposite end. Its dual nature reflects the Dagda's role as a god of both destruction and creation, abundance and wisdom. The club's ability to both slay and revive underscores the cyclical nature of existence, a central theme in Celtic belief. It is a potent symbol of the Dagda's sovereignty and his mastery over the forces of nature and fate. Legends say the Dagda used it to stir cauldrons of plenty and to defend his people against formidable foes. Its very presence commanded respect and awe, a testament to the Dagda's unparalleled authority.
subtitle: A mighty club of life and death.
origin: Forged by the gods themselves or found in a primordial realm, its origins are shrouded in myth.
currentLocation: Lost to time, its whereabouts are currently unknown. Some believe it lies hidden in a sacred grove or buried beneath an ancient cairn.

keyMyths.title: The Second Battle of Mag Tuired
keyMyths.description: During the Second Battle of Mag Tuired, the Dagda wielded his club with devastating effect against the Fomorians, striking down countless enemies and turning the tide of the battle in favor of the Tuatha Dé Danann. His powerful strikes created craters in the earth and shook the very foundations of the world. It was a key factor in their victory.
keyMyths.source: The Second Battle of Mag Tuired

keyMyths.title: The Dagda's Feast
keyMyths.description: The Dagda, using his club, stirred a cauldron of immense size, filled with porridge made with the carcasses of oxen and swine. This cauldron provided sustenance for the Tuatha Dé Danann, demonstrating the Dagda's role as a provider and protector of his people. This act symbolized his role as a god of abundance and sustenance.

keyMyths.title: The Dagda and the Morrígan
keyMyths.description: In some accounts, the Dagda used his club to defend himself against the Morrígan, the goddess of war and fate. Although their relationship was complex and often adversarial, the Dagda's strength and the power of his club allowed him to hold his own against her formidable abilities. This highlighted his power even against formidable foes.
keyMyths.source: D'Arbois de Jubainville, H. (1903). The Irish Mythological Cycle and Celtic Mythology.

sources.author: Macalister, R.A.S.
sources.work: Lebor na hUidre: Book of the Dun Cow
sources.description: A primary source containing early Irish mythological narratives, providing context for the Dagda's role and attributes.
sources.text: A primary source containing early Irish mythological narratives, providing context for the Dagda's role and attributes.

sources.author: O'Rahilly, T.F.
sources.description: Provides an analysis of the Dagda's character and functions within the broader context of Irish mythology and social structure.
sources.text: Provides an analysis of the Dagda's character and functions within the broader context of Irish mythology and social structure.

sources.author: Green, Miranda
sources.work: Myths and Symbols in Pagan Europe: Early Scandinavian and Celtic Religions
sources.description: Explores the symbolism associated with the Dagda and his club, placing them within the broader context of Celtic religious beliefs.
